coredata.dc:title: Novel solution- and paper-based sensors based on label-free fluorescent carbon dots for the selective detection of pyrimethanil
coredata.dc:creator: Supchocksoonthorn, Preeyanuch
Hanchaina, Rattanavinan
Sinoy, Ma Concepcion Alvior
de Luna, Mark Daniel G.
Kangsamaksin, Thaned
Paoprasert, Peerasak
coredata.dc:description: Novel sensors based on fluorescent carbon dots were developed for a rapid, selective, and sensitive detection of pyrimethanil, a fungicide widely used in many agricultural crops. The carbon dots were synthesized from maleic anhydride and triethylenetetramine via a facile one-pot pyrolysis. A quenched fluorescence intensity of carbon dots in the presence of pyrimethanil occurred via inner filter effect and strong π-π interaction. The carbon dots were sensitive to pyrimethanil across a linear range from 0.5 to 75 µM with a low detection limit of 14 nM. The sensing results could be generated in 30 s. The fluorescent carbon dots showed excellent stability and selectivity to pyrimethanil in the presence of interferences, including fungicides and metal ions. They were also successfully employed for pyrimethanil detection in apples, cucumbers, and drinking water, yielding 98.6–107.1% recovery. The practical, portable, and reusable paper-based sensor was also developed. The cytotoxicity of carbon dots was tested to demonstrate that they are sufficiently safe to use. The novel pyrimethanil sensor based on fluorescent carbon dots developed in this work can be used as a high-performance sensor and a rapid tool for quality control in food, agricultural, and environmental monitoring.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Pyrimethanil
Maleic anhydride
Triethylenetetramine
Fluorescence sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of carbon dots from MA and TETA
Synthesis and characterization of carbon dots
Effects of pH, time, and temperature

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Department of Biotechnology, Faculty of Science and Technology
Thammasat University Center of Scientific Equipment for Advanced Research

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This project is funded by National Research Council of Thailand (NRCT5-RSA63010-03) and the Royal Golden Jubilee Research Fund. Financial support from the Thailand Research Fund through the Royal Golden Jubilee Ph.D. Program (Grant No. PHD/0130/2559) was provided to P. Supchocksoonthorn and P. Paoprasert. The authors gratefully acknowledge Mahidol University under the New Discovery and Frontier Research Grant and use of facilities and instrumentation supported by the Central Scientific Instrument Center (CSIC), Thammasat University Center of Scientific Equipment for Advanced Research (TUCSEAR), Department of Chemistry, Department of Biotechnology, Faculty of Science and Technology, and Thammasat University.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme of the formation of carbon dots (CDs) derived from maleic anhydride (MA) and triethylenetetramine (TETA) for the detection of pyrimethanil.
(a) TEM image; inset figure shows a high-resolution image and lattice spacing and (b) histogram of size distribution of carbon dots.

FT-IR spectra of MA (red line), TETA (blue line), and carbon dots (CDs, black line). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(a) Survey XPS spectrum, and high-resolution XPS spectra of (b) C1s, (c) O1s, and (d) N1s regions of carbon dots.

(a) Excitation-dependence fluorescence emission spectra and (b) UV–vis absorbance spectrum and the maximum fluorescence emission spectrum of carbon dots; inset figures show the carbon dot solutions under room light and 365-nm UV light.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of carbon dots without (blue line) and with (black line) 100 µM of PMT at pH 1; inset figures show the carbon dot (CD) solution (left) and carbon dot/PMT solution (right) under 365-nm UV light. (b) UV–vis spectrum of PMT and fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of carbon dots. (c) Tauc plots of carbon dots (blue line) and PMT (red line). (d) Scheme of energy level diagram describing competitive absorption and energy transfer processes. (e) Scheme showing the interactions between carbon dots and PMT. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Plot of ratio of fluorescence intensities versus pH and (b) UV–vis absorbance spectra of PMT in different pH values and fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of carbon dots. Plots of ratio of fluorescence intensities versus (c) mixing time and (d) temperature.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of carbon dots in the presence of PMT with different concentrations at pH 1. (b) The plot between F0/F and concentration of PMT; inset figure shows the linear range of the plot.

HPLC chromatograms of standard PMT and real samples. Inset shows the fluorescence spectra of the real samples containing PMT (left) and the calibration curve of peak areas versus concentrations of PMT (right).

Fluorescence emission spectra of carbon dots in the presence of interferences (a) with and (b) without PMT (control sample is the pure carbon dot solution). The relative fluorescence intensities in percentage of carbon dots (CDs) in the presence of interference (c) with and (d) without PMT.

(a) Images of carbon dot-paper with the application of PMT. (b) Plot of RGB values and concentrations of PMT. Inset plot shows the RGB0/RGB values versus the concentrations of PMT. (c) Images and RGB values of the carbon dot-paper as a function of cycle number. (d) The relative RGB values in percentage of the carbon dot-paper as a function of cycle number.

(a) Microscope images of HeLa cells treated with different concentrations of carbon dots for 24 h. (b) Relative cell viability versus concentration of carbon dots. ns is non-significant. Statistically significant (p < 0.001) changes are indicated by symbol ***.
Comparison of sensing platforms for the detection of PMT.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: GCNF = graphitized carbon nanofiber, rGO = reduced graphene oxide, IL = ionic liquid, MWCNTs = multi-walled carbon nanotubes, TiC/C = carbon nanofiber supported titanium carbide, β-CD = β-cyclodextrin, and GCE = glassy carbon electrode.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of PMT in real samples using fluorescence and HPLC techniques.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Tabulated t- and F- values at P = 0.05 are 2.78 and 19.00, respectively.

head.ce:title: Novel solution- and paper-based sensors based on label-free fluorescent carbon dots for the selective detection of pyrimethanil

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science and Technology, Thammasat University, Pathumthani 12120, Thailand
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science and Technology, Thammasat University, Pathumthani 12120, Thailand

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biochemistry, Faculty of Science, Mahidol University, Bangkok 10400, Thailand
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Biochemistry, Faculty of Science, Mahidol University, Bangkok 10400, Thailand

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, University of the Philippines-Diliman, Quezon City 1101, Philippines
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering, University of the Philippines-Diliman, Quezon City 1101, Philippines

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Novel sensors based on fluorescent carbon dots were developed for a rapid, selective, and sensitive detection of pyrimethanil, a fungicide widely used in many agricultural crops. The carbon dots were synthesized from maleic anhydride and triethylenetetramine via a facile one-pot pyrolysis. A quenched fluorescence intensity of carbon dots in the presence of pyrimethanil occurred via inner filter effect and strong π-π interaction. The carbon dots were sensitive to pyrimethanil across a linear range from 0.5 to 75 µM with a low detection limit of 14 nM. The sensing results could be generated in 30 s. The fluorescent carbon dots showed excellent stability and selectivity to pyrimethanil in the presence of interferences, including fungicides and metal ions. They were also successfully employed for pyrimethanil detection in apples, cucumbers, and drinking water, yielding 98.6–107.1% recovery. The practical, portable, and reusable paper-based sensor was also developed. The cytotoxicity of carbon dots was tested to demonstrate that they are sufficiently safe to use. The novel pyrimethanil sensor based on fluorescent carbon dots developed in this work can be used as a high-performance sensor and a rapid tool for quality control in food, agricultural, and environmental monitoring.

ce:section.ce:para: Despite their threats, fungicides are important for global food security. They are widely utilized to protect plants and agricultural crops from fungi which can cause damage, limit growth, and result in poor product quality [1]. Pyrimethanil (4,6-dimethyl-N-phenyl-2-pyrimidinamine, PMT) is an organic fungicide in the anilinopyrimidine class. It is one of the most used fungicides, particularly against gray mold on agricultural products, such as apples, strawberries, and cucumbers [2]. The United States Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) conducted a study and found PMT residue in several fruits and vegetables [3]. The residue level is generally reported as the maximum residue limit (MRL, mg/kg). According to the European Food Safety Authority (EFSA), the acceptable daily intake of PMT is 0.17 mg/kg of body weight [4]. Overexposure to a high dose of PMT could damage internal organs, cause respiratory paralysis, or lead to death [2,5]. Therefore, developing sensitive, rapid, and reliable analytical methods to detect PMT is necessary to protect our health.

There are many available techniques for detecting fungicides, including gas chromatography [6], high-performance liquid chromatography [7], liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry [8], surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy [9], and capillary electrophoresis [10]. However, these techniques involve complex sample preparation, expensive equipment, and have limited capability for real-time and on-site detection. Fluorescence spectroscopy has attracted attention because it provides cheap, ultrasensitive, simple, portable, and rapid measurements [11]. Fluorescent materials or fluorophores must be particularly selective to fungicides and stable under various working conditions. They must also provide a high level of sensitivity and low toxicity for practical uses [12].

Carbon dots have received wide attention in sensing applications because they are optically unique nanomaterials with outstanding fluorescence, excellent photostability, biocompatibility, high aqueous solubility, and straightforward preparation [13–15]. Their structures possess functional groups on the surface of carbon cores, which provide sites to interact with ions and chemicals. The interactions between carbon dots and analytes can produce fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), photo-induced electron transfer (PET), and inner filter effect (IFE) leading to altered optical properties of carbon dots [16–18]. Carbon dots have been employed as fungicide sensors. For example, Wang et al. reported the use of amine-terminated carbon dots and Cu2+ ion for the detection of thiram with a low detection limit of 0.75 µg·mL−1 on the membrane of fungal cells. In the absence of thiram, the fluorescence emission of amine-terminated carbon dots with Cu2+ ion reduced via PET. However, in the presence of thiram, the fluorescence emission of carbon dot-NH2 further decreased because the Cu2+-thiram complex could absorb both excitation and emission energy of amine-terminated carbon dots via IFE [19]. Yang et al. reported the use of nitrogen-doped carbon dots synthesized from m-phenylenediamine and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) for the detection of carbendazim with a low detection limit of 0.002 µM. The fluorescence emission of nitrogen-doped carbon dots in the presence of AuNPs was quenched via FRET. However, the fluorescence emission of carbon dots recovered after adding carbendazim due to the strong interaction between AuNPs and carbendazim [20]. Zou et al. reported a simple use of pristine nitrogen-doped carbon dots from L-cysteine for the detection of fluazinam. The carbon dots formed π-π interactions and hydrogen bonding with fluazinam, leading to fluorescence quenching and sensitive detection with a detection limit of 10 nM. Moreover, the paper-based sensor was fabricated to demonstrate the practicality of the carbon dot probes [21]. From these studies, the carbon dots have shown their capability as fluorescent probes for the detection of fungicides. However, the carbon dots have never been explored as a PMT sensor. Although some materials have been developed for PMT detection, including graphene oxide [22], carbon nanotube [23], gold nanoparticle [9], and titanium carbide [24], these materials are expensive and tedious to prepare and were based on electrochemical techniques. Therefore, the development of carbon dots as fluorescent probes for PMT sensing is attractive because of their cost-effectiveness, facile synthesis, portability, and outstanding selectivity towards analytes.

Herein, a novel PMT sensor based on label-free fluorescent carbon dots was developed (Fig. 1 ). The nitrogen-rich carbon dots were synthesized from maleic anhydride (MA) and triethylenetetramine (TETA) as carbon and nitrogen sources through a one-step pyrolysis. The nitrogen-rich carbon dots were employed as PMT probes. The effects of PMT concentration, pH, time, and temperature on the photoluminescence of carbon dots were investigated. The detection of PMT using carbon dots in apples, cucumbers, drinking water, and with interfering substances was studied. A paper-based sensor was also fabricated to test practicality and reusability. The cell viability of the carbon dots was measured using 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay. In summary, the novel sensor from carbon dots for PMT sensing was found to be simple and effective. The effectiveness of the sensor lies upon the rapid, sensitive, and selective detection of PMT, which can be practically applied to control the quality and safety of our foods.

All reagents used were at least analytical reagent grade. MA, TETA, and quinine sulfate were obtained from Acros Organics. AlCl3, CaCl2, Cd (NO3)2, CoCl2, CuBr, CuSO4·5H2O, KCl, Li2SO4, LiCl, LiNO3, MgCl2, Na2SO4, NiCl2, PbCl2, SnCl2, ZnCl2, tebuconazole, vinclozolin, 2,2′-methylenebis(4-chlorophenol) or dichlorophen, and PMT were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. Acetonitrile (HPLC grade) from Carlo Erba was used for high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC). Apple, cucumber, and drinking water were purchased from a local market. A dialysis membrane, with a molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) of 1,000 Da, was bought from Membrane Filtration Products, Inc. All other chemicals including nitric acid (96% w/w) and ethanol were obtained from Carlo Erba. The PMT stock solution was prepared in ethanol while others were prepared in deionized (DI) water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon dots from MA and TETA
ce:section.ce:para: Briefly, 4.0 g of MA and 2.0 g of TETA were dissolved in 30 mL DI water. The mixture was placed in a crucible with 20 mL of 2.5 M HNO3 and pyrolyzed at 250 °C for 4 h using a temperature-controlled furnace. The reaction mixture was cooled at room temperature, diluted with DI water to 100 mL, and placed in an ultrasonicator for 30 min. The mixture was then filtered using a filter paper (pore size: 11 µm). The filtrate was neutralized using 2.5 M NaOH before it was dialyzed using a dialysis membrane for 24 h. The solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min. The supernatant was collected and freeze-dried to obtain a very light yellowish solid (2.83 mg, 0.05%).

The quantum yield of the carbon dots was quantified based on previous studies [25,26]. Quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 solution was used as a reference. The absorbance and fluorescence peak areas of different concentrations of carbon dots and quinine sulfate were measured (quantum yield = 54%, excitation at 349 nm). The quantum yield of the carbon dots was calculated using the slope method.

Size and morphology of the carbon dots were observed using a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (TEM, TecnaiTM G2 F20, FEI Corporation). The optical absorption and fluorescence emission spectra were measured using a Duetta fluorescence and absorbance spectrometer (HORIBA). The chemical composition of the carbon dots was analyzed by a FT-IR spectrometer (Spectrum 2000) and an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS; AXIS ULTRADLD, Kratos Analytical, Manchester UK). The cell viability was measured using a microplate reader (Multiscan EX, Thermo Labsystems).

The PMT stock solution was prepared in ethanol (200 µM, 50 mL) whereas the carbon dot solution was prepared in DI water (0.5 mg·mL−1). The carbon dots and PMT solutions were mixed in the ratio of 1:1 to a total volume of 6 mL, and then the pH was adjusted using HCl and NaOH. The fluorescence intensities were recorded at 400 nm with an excitation wavelength of 320 nm in the range from 340 to 550 nm. The fluorescence intensity of pure carbon dot solution was measured and set as F0 . Meanwhile, the corresponding fluorescence intensities of the analyte solutions with carbon dots were recorded as F. A series of F/F0 and F0/F values were used in y-axis of the plots.

To investigate reliability and accuracy of the fluorescence method for the detection of PMT, apples, cucumbers, and drinking water were chosen for the real sample test. 20 g of apple and cucumber pericarps were cut into small pieces, immersed in 60 mL ethanol, and then ultrasonicated for 60 min. The solutions were filtered using a filter paper (pore size: 11 µm) and then a 0.22-µm nylon filter. In the case of the drinking water sample, 20 mL of water was added into 60 mL ethanol. All the above solutions were kept at 4 °C in a refrigerator for future use. The standard PMT solutions (5.0, 10.0, 25.0, 50.0, 75.0, and 100 µM) and real samples spiked with the standard PMT solution (0.0, 10.0, 25.0, and 50.0 µM) were prepared and then filtered through 0.22-µm nylon filter. The amount of PMT concentration was determined using a fluorescence spectrometer and high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC). The HPLC system (Shimadzu, LC-20AT series) with the UV detector (268 nm), C18 column (4.6 mm i.d. × 150 mm, 5 µm particle diameter) joined with a guard column (4.6 mm i.d. × 12.5 mm, 5 µm particle diameter) and acetonitrile/water mixture (1:1 v/v) as a mobile phase were used. The solutions were injected into a 10-µL feeding loop of the HPLC at ambient temperature with a flow rate of 1.0 mL·min−1. t-Test and F-test were performed to examine the validity of the results.

In order to test the interfering effects of common metal ions and other fungicide chemicals towards carbon dots in the presence of PMT, the fluorescence intensities of the carbon dots with 100 µM PMT were measured in the presence of 100 µM interferences, including Al3+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cu+, Cu2+, K+, Li+, Cl-,
N O
3
-
, Mg2+, Na+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Sn2+, Zn2+, tebuconazole, vinclozolin, and dichlorophen under the optimized condition. The carbon dot solutions were also measured in the presence of 1,000 µM interfering substances and compared with the carbon dot solution without adding the interfering substances.

A paper-based sensor is an analytical platform that provides a portable and on-site detection. In this study, cellulose based Whatman filter papers were cut into squares of approximately 1 × 1 cm2. Each piece of paper was immersed in the carbon dot solution (0.5 mg·mL−1) and dried at 60 °C for 2 h. Different concentrations (0, 1.0, 5.0, 10.0, 25.0, 50.0, 75.0, 100.0, 200.0, and 300.0 µM, 0.1 mL each) of PMT were dropped onto the carbon dot-coated filter paper (carbon dot-paper). The reusability of carbon dot-paper in the presence of PMT was also studied. 0.1 mL of 100 µM PMT solution was dropped onto the carbon dot-paper and then the fluorescence emission was measured. After that, the PMT on the carbon dot-paper was removed by immersing in ethanol solution for 30 s and the fluorescence emission was measured again. This cycle was repeated. The changes in fluorescence emission under 365-nm UV light were investigated using a digital phone camera (iPhone SE2) and the color shades of the digital images were analyzed using Photoshop (Adobe®). The sensitivity of the carbon dot-paper was calculated from the sum red–greenblue (RGB) values using Eq. (1) (n = 5):
(1)
Sum
RGB
values = Red + Green + Blue

Cytotoxicity of carbon dots was examined using MTT assay and HeLa cells. The HeLa cells were grown in High Glucose Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM, Gibco) supplemented with 10% heat-inactivated serum (FBS, Gibco) and 1X Pen-Strep (Gibco) in standard incubator conditions (37 °C, 5% CO2). The HeLa cells (5 × 104 cells/well) were then incubated in a 24-well plate for 24 h. The solutions of carbon dots with different concentrations (0.00, 0.25, 0.50, and 1.00 mg·mL−1) were added in triplicate in the 24-well plate. After that, the medium was removed and 500 µL MTT (0.5 mg·mL−1) was added into each well and incubated for additional 3 h. Then, excess medium was removed and dimethyl sulfoxide was replaced. The absorbance of the colored cells was measured at 540 nm using a microplate reader. The cell viability was calculated using Eq. (2) (n = 3):
(2)
C e l l
v i a b i l i t y
%
=
OD treated
OD control
× 100

where ODtreated and ODcontrol are the absorbance (optical density) of the colored cells treated with and without carbon dots. The IC50 value (half maximal inhibitory concentration) was determined from the MTT results using GraphPad Prism software.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: The carbon dots were synthesized from MA and TETA. MA is an important chemical building block and precursor in many compounds. TETA is a nitrogen-rich compound and has also been used for the synthesis of carbon dots. The nitrogen atoms in TETA can efficiently enhance the fluorescence emission of carbon dots [27]. Fan et al. reported the preparation of carbon dots with a high quantum yield (11.4%) using citric acid and TETA as a passivating agent via thermal pyrolysis [28]. MA and TETA are therefore suitable starting materials for the synthesis of carbon dots. They were successfully carbonized via a one-step pyrolysis with an addition of nitric acid at 250 °C for 4 h. Nitric acid can also act as nitrogen source and oxidant [29]. It can increase nitrogen and oxygen-functional groups on the surface of the carbon dots.

Size and morphology of the carbon dots were evaluated using TEM. The synthesized carbon dots are quasi-spherical in shape with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm and well dispersed as shown in Fig. 2 a and the corresponding nanoparticle size distribution histogram displayed an average size of 5.9 nm (Fig. 2b).

XPS analysis was performed to investigate the compositional elements and functional groups of carbon dots. The XPS survey spectrum displayed the characteristic peaks of carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen elements at 284.8, 531.8, and 399.8 eV with the atomic concentrations of 40.22, 40.00, and 4.95%, respectively (Fig. 4 a) [37]. The high-resolution spectrum of C1s showed three peaks at 285.0, 286.5, and 288.5 eV, which indicated the presence of C-C, C-OH, and C=O, respectively (Fig. 4b) [38]. The high-resolution spectrum of O1s exhibited four peaks at 531.6, 532.5, 534.0, and 535.8 eV, which are attributed to the C-OH, C-O-C, C=O (aromatic), and C=O bonds (Fig. 4c) [39,40]. The high-resolution scan of the N1s showed the presence of C-NH2, pyrrolic nitrogen, N-H, and O-NO2 at 399.3, 400.5, 401.8, and 407.0 eV (Fig. 4d) [38,41]. The presence of O-NO2 groups is possibly because of the use of nitric acid in the synthesis. Both FT-IR and XPS results verified that the carbon dots are highly rich in carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen. The dominant pyrrolic nitrogen peak also suggests the presence of aromatic pyrrole rings in the carbon dots [42,43].

The optical properties of the carbon dots were evaluated by fluorescence and UV–vis spectroscopy. An excitation-dependent emission with the excitation wavelength from 280 to 460 nm and a red-shift of the emission wavelength with increasing excitation wavelength were observed (Fig. 5 a). The excitation-dependent behavior is caused by various emissive states [44]. The maximum emission wavelength at 400 nm was obtained under an excitation wavelength of 320 nm with a fluorescence quantum yield of 6.3%. Under the 365-nm UV light, the carbon dot solution exhibited a bright blue color emission (inset of Fig. 5b). The UV–vis spectrum revealed a strong absorption peak at 285 nm which is contributed by the π – π* transition of the C=C bonds of localized sp2 clusters in the core (Fig. 5b) [35]. This suggests that the carbon dots possessed a large aromatic core. Li et al. described that the UV–vis absorption band peak of 250–300 nm region indicates a typical absorption of an aromatic π-system with carbon dot diameter in the range of 5–10 nm [45]. The average size of our carbon dots of 5.9 nm and the strong pyrrolic peak in XPS spectrum support the statement in Li et al. work. The UV–vis absorption and fluorescence emission of carbon dots reflect not only the particle size and aromatic π-system but also the distribution of different emissive sites on the carbon dot surface.

In this work, PMT sensor was first explored using carbon dots as fluorescent probes. A solution containing carbon dots and PMT was taken for fluorescence emission measurement and compared with the pure carbon dot solution. The fluorescence signal of carbon dots significantly reduced with the addition of PMT, indicating a non-radiative interaction between PMT and carbon dots (Fig. 6 a). The conversion from bright blue color to dark blue color of carbon dots in solution was clearly observed in the presence of PMT as shown in the inset of Fig. 6a. Since PMT is an aromatic compound and has strong UV–vis absorption, we then investigated the inner filter effect (IFE) between carbon dots and PMT by measuring the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of carbon dots and UV–vis absorption of PMT as shown in Fig. 6b. The IFE can take place via two pathways: 1) the competitive energy absorption between donor and acceptor and 2) the transfer of emission energy from donor to acceptor [46]. The first pathway was confirmed by the overlap between the excitation spectrum of carbon dots and PMT absorption between 280 and 350 nm (red area), resulting in a less excitation to the carbon dots (Fig. 6b and d). The second pathway was also confirmed by the overlap between carbon dot emission and PMT absorption between 320 and 380 nm (blue area). This led to a reduced fluorescence emission of the carbon dots. A combination of less excitation and energy transfer thus led to a fluorescence quenching of the carbon dots in the presence of PMT. To further confirm the IFE between carbon dots and PMT, Tauc plot was constructed to estimate the optical band gap energy of the carbon dots and PMT (Fig. 6c). The optical band gap energy (Eg) was determined by plotting (
α h v )
r
versus
hv
, where
α , h ,
and v are the absorption coefficient, Planck’s constant, and light frequency, respectively, and r is 2 for a direct band gap material (supplementary materials) [47]. The optical band gaps of carbon dots and PMT were determined to be 3.80 and 3.52 eV, respectively. The similar and slightly larger optical band gap of carbon dots than that of PMT suggests that the competitive energy absorption was plausible and the emission energy from the carbon dots was enough to excite the PMT. From these results, we can conclude that the favorable IFE led to a significant reduction in fluorescence emission of the carbon dots in the presence of PMT. A strong band at 285 nm corresponds to the π – π* transition of the C=C bonds of localized sp2 clusters and the FT-IR and XPS spectra of carbon dots exhibited the presence of C=C/C=O groups. The π-π interaction between a large aromatic core of the carbon dots and anilinopyrimidine unit of PMT and between their polar functional groups brought them to close proximity, which makes the IFE effective (Fig. 6e).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of pH, time, and temperature
ce:section.ce:para: Effects of pH on fluorescence quenching were investigated. The fluorescence intensities of carbon dots in the presence of PMT at different pH values from pH 1 to 13 were measured (Fig. 7 a). The F/F0 intensities increased dramatically from pH 1 to 5 but they leveled off between pH 7 and 13. This indicates that strong acidic conditions produced the highest fluorescence quenching. To explain these results, the UV–vis absorption spectra of PMT in pH 1, 5, and 13 were measured and compared with the fluorescence emission and excitation spectra of carbon dots (Fig. 7b). It was found that the overlapped area between UV–vis spectrum of PMT and fluorescence emission and excitation spectra of carbon dots in pH 1 was the largest, indicating the most effective IFE as compared to other pH values. This further confirms that IFE was responsible for the fluorescence quenching of carbon dots by PMT. Therefore, pH 1 was chosen for all subsequent experiments.

The effects of mixing time on the fluorescence quenching were studied. It was observed that the F/F0 values were not significantly changed from 30 s to 10 min, meaning that the fluorescence quenching was completed immediately after mixing because 30 s were actually the time required for a fluorescence spectrum scan (Fig. 7c). This indicates that our carbon dots could detect the PMT and rapidly produce detection results in 30 s or less. The fast fluorescent quenching is possibly due to an efficient IFE and strong interactions between carbon dots and PMT. The long-term stability of the carbon dots in solution was studied by measuring their fluorescence emission at different time intervals. The fluorescence intensity at 400 nm only slightly decreased over time and they remained above 85% after 2 months (Fig. S1). The fluorescence sensitivity based on the temperature was evaluated to determine whether or not the fluorescence quenching was via static or dynamic mechanism (Fig. 7d). The carbon dot solutions in the presence and absence of PMT at different temperatures (20, 25, 40, 60, and 80 °C) were measured. The fluorescence intensity increased with increasing temperature. This result confirmed the static quenching was a dominant mechanism because an increase in temperature decreased the stability of carbon dot-PMT complex, leading to a recovery of fluorescence emission [48]. A dynamic fluorescence quenching would otherwise be enhanced with increasing temperature due to more frequent collisions. These results also confirmed the strong π-π interactions between carbon dots and PMT, resulting in the formation of static complexes.

To evaluate the analytical performance of the carbon dots, the fluorescence emission of mixtures between seventeen concentrations of PMT (0.5, 1.0, 3.0, 5.0, 10.0, 15.0, 30.0, 40.0, 50.0, 75.0, 80.0, 90.0, 100.0, 200.0, 300.0, 400.0, and 500.0 µM) and carbon dots (0.5 mg·mL−1) was measured. The fluorescence emission of carbon dots decreased with increasing concentration of PMT (Fig. 8 a). The Stern-Volmer plot of F0/F values versus concentration of PMT was shown in Fig. 8b. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated using 3
σ / s l o p e
where σ is the standard deviation of fluorescence intensity without adding PMT (n = 5) and the slope was obtained from the fitting equation: F0/F = 0.0125x + 0.9709 (R2 = 0.9907). The LOD value was found to be 14 nM. From the plot, the linear range was determined to be 0.5–75 µM. According to the maximum residue level (MRL) reported by the European Food Safety Authority, the acceptable daily intake is 0.17 mg/kg or 0.85 µM [4]. Therefore, our fluorescence probe is capable of detecting PMT at much below the harmful concentration. Moreover, according to other sensing platforms for the detection of PMT as shown in Table 1 , the performance of our carbon dots was among the best, and to the best of our knowledge, it is also the first fluorescence-based method.

The selectivity of carbon dots towards PMT was investigated. An interference test was studied using the following metal ions and fungicides as interferences: Al3+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cu+, Cu2+, K+, Li+, Cl-,
N O
3
-
, Mg2+, Na+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Sn2+, Zn2+, tebuconazole, vinclozolin, and dichlorophen. These chosen metals are commonly found in agricultural products, such as fruits and vegetables, and in soil, water, and nutrient solutions [51]. Tebuconazole, vinclozolin, and dichlorophen were chosen for the interference test because they are fungicides often contaminated in fruits, such as apples [52] and contain aromatic units similar to PMT [53–55]. Two sets of solutions of carbon dots and interferences were prepared. The first set contained the carbon dots, interferences, and PMT while the second set contained only carbon dots and interferences. For the first set, the fluorescence emission of all solutions significantly dropped with respect to the pure carbon dot solution (Fig. 10 a) and the relative fluorescence intensities in percentage were in the range 95.6–100.1% (Fig. 10c). For the second set, the fluorescence emission of all solutions was nearly the same as that of the pure carbon dot solution (Fig. 10b). This indicates that metal ions and other fungicides could not quench the carbon dots and only PMT could effectively quench the carbon dots despite the presence of interferences. A tenfold increase in concentration of interferences did not affect the fluorescence intensity of carbon dots (Fig. 10d). Based on all of these results, the carbon dots were highly selective to PMT against various interferences.

Paper-based sensor is a simple and convenient sensing platform designed for portable and on-site testing. In this study, the paper-based sensor was fabricated using a filter paper coated with the carbon dot solution (carbon dot-paper). The PMT solutions (1.0, 5.0, 10.0, 25.0, 50.0, 75.0, 100.0, 200.0, and 300.0 µM) were then dropped on the carbon dot-paper and illuminated using a 365-nm UV lamp and images were taken of (Fig. 11 a). As the PMT concentration was increased, the blue color of the images became darker, indicating that increasing PMT concentration led to more fluorescence reduction. The Stern-Volmer relationship between sum RGB values extracted from the images and the PMT concentrations was depicted in Fig. 11b. From the plot, a linear range from 1.0 to 100.0 µM was obtained and the limit of detection was found to be 0.8 µM (3 σ /slope), which is sensitive enough to detect the maximum concentration allowed for daily intake. To test the reusability of the carbon dot-paper, the following procedure was performed. The PMT was dropped on the carbon dot-paper and then removed by immersing in pure ethanol because PMT is soluble in ethanol. The images were taken of before and after PMT removal and the process was repeated. The sum RGB values of the carbon dot-paper was found to reduce slightly as the cycle number increased as shown in Fig. 11c. This means that the carbon dots on the paper were gradually removed. However, the RGB values of carbon dot-paper remained above 90% after eight repeated cycles (Fig. 11d). In addition, the application of PMT on the carbon dot-paper still produced an obvious fluorescence quenching and reduction in RGB values, indicating that the paper sensor can be reused for PMT detection. There is still a clear difference of RGB values between carbon dot-paper after PMT application and removal after eight cycles. Although more cycles could be performed, we stopped after the eighth cycle because the paper texture started to degrade. This study still illustrates that the carbon dot-paper with high sensitivity and reusability can be simply prepared by just soaking the filter paper with the carbon dot solution and demonstrates the potential as portable devices for on-site testing. The carbon dot-paper can be reused for eight times, which to the best of our knowledge is the best [56]. Strong binding came from the interactions between the highly polar carbon dots and cellulose in filter paper. Further improvement in reusability can be simply performed by covalent grafting and the use of binder and more durable papers, which can be done as future work.

Carbon dots are widely regarded as biocompatible nanomaterials. To determine whether or not the carbon dots from MA and TETA are biocompatible and can be safely used or handled for PMT detection, the cytotoxicity of the carbon dots was examined. MTT assay using HeLa cells was performed to investigate the cytotoxicity of carbon dots. The microscope images of the HeLa cells treated with different concentrations of carbon dots (0.00, 0.25, 0.50, and 1.00 mg‧mL−1) for 24 h were shown in Fig. 12 a. The HeLa cells in the absence of carbon dots were used as the control. The cells in carbon dot solutions (0.25 and 0.50 mg‧mL−1) after 24 h incubation showed no significant change in shape, indicating low cytotoxicity of carbon dots. The 1.0 mg‧mL−1 carbon dot solution, however, reduced the number of live cells. The relative cell viability was calculated to be 97.0, 97.4, and 53.6% for 0.25, 0.50, and 1.00 mg‧mL−1 carbon dots, respectively (Fig. 12b). The IC50 was calculated to be 1.37 mg‧mL−1. A high concentration of carbon dots enhanced the toxicity to the cells because of the nitrogen containing functional groups on the carbon dots. The reactive nitrogen specie such as alkyl peroxynitriles (RONO2) can lead to depolarization of mitochondrial membrane [57]. From the XPS spectrum, the O-NO2 groups were observed, which could be the cause of cytotoxicity of carbon dots at high concentrations. However, from the sensing experiments the 0.5 mg‧mL−1 carbon dot solution was used throughout and found to be excellent for PMT sensing, and this concentration is well below the IC50 and gave 97.4% cell viability. This result indicated that our carbon dot probes are sufficiently biocompatible and safe for using and handling and also have potential for other applications.

This research shows the high sensing performance of carbon dots synthesized from MA and TETA as carbon and nitrogen sources via pyrolysis process. The carbon dots were demonstrated to be good fluorescence probes with high sensitivity, selectivity, practicality, and reusability for a fast detection of PMT. Regarding to sensitivity and fast detection, the carbon dots could detect PMT with a very low detection limit of 14 nM and also produce the detection results within 30 s. They were also selective to PMT despite the presence of a range of interferences even at a ten-fold concentration. The paper-based sensor was fabricated and demonstrated the practicality of on-site testing. Furthermore, the paper-based sensor exhibited excellent reusability as it could be reused for at least eight times and still be able to detect PMT. The concentrations of PMT in apples, cucumbers, and drinking water were accurately determined. The high sensitivity and selectivity detection of PMT by carbon dots occurred via IFE and strong π-π interactions. The carbon dots were also stable up to 2 months in solution, indicating a long shelf-life. The carbon dots also showed low cytotoxicity and therefore safe for uses and handling. Based on these results, the carbon dots from MA and TETA proved to be potent PMT sensor with a wide range of applications in food quality and environmental control.

Preeyanuch Supchocksoonthorn: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft. Rattanavinan Hanchaina: Methodology, Investigation. Ma Concepcion Alvior Sinoy: Methodology, Investigation. Mark Daniel G. Luna: Supervision. Thaned Kangsamaksin: Supervision. Peerasak Paoprasert: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Writing - review & editing.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Banerjee, H. Bairagi, S. Mukhopadhyay, A. Pal, D. Bera, L. Ray, Heavy metal contamination in fruits and vegetables in two districts of West Bengal, India, Elec. J. Env. Agricult. Food Chem., 9 (2010) 1423-1432.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Dissipation kinetics of trifloxystrobin and tebuconazole on apple (Malus domestica) and soil – a multi location study from north western Himalayan region
Chemosphere

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.K. Patyal, I.D. Sharma, R.S. Chandel, J.K. Dubey, Dissipation kinetics of trifloxystrobin and tebuconazole on apple (Malus domestica) and soil – a multi location study from north western Himalayan region, Chemosphere, 92 (2013) 949-954.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: J.D. Lović, D.Ž. Mijin, M.B. Jovanović, O.S. Glavaški, T.M. Zeremski, S.D. Petrović, M.L. Avramov Ivić, An investigation of tebuconazole degradation using a gold electrode, C. R. Chim., 19 (2016) 639-645.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Role of metabolism in the endocrine-disrupting effects of chemicals in aquatic and terrestrial systems
Pure Appl. Chem.

Mol.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Liu, Y. Xiao, J.-Q. Li, B. Fu, Z. Qin, 1,1-Diaryl compounds as important bioactive module in pesticides, Mol., 23 (2019) 809-820.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Dual function based on switchable colorimetric luminescence for water and temperature sensing in two-dimensional metal–organic framework nanosheets
ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces

The journal of venomous animals and toxins including tropical diseases
